transformation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-29T13:43:20Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T08:23:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi guys, I really appreciate your amazing work that goes into LFortran. I thought, I'll test, how far I get with it to build the [Fortuno unit testing](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno) framework, to give further impulses for the development. :smile: 

I encountered a segfault when compiling the a given source file. The kind of minimal reproducer seems to be:
```fortran
module fortuno_basetypes
  implicit none

  type, abstract :: test_base
    character(:), allocatable :: name
  end type test_base


  type :: test_ptr_item
    class(test_base), pointer :: item => null()                                 
  end type test_ptr_item


  type :: test_list
    private
    type(test_ptr_item), pointer :: storage_(:) => null()                       
    integer :: nitems = 0
  contains
    procedure :: free => test_list_free
  end type


contains

  subroutine test_list_free(this)
    class(test_list), intent(inout) :: this                                     

    select type (item => this%storage_(1)%item)                                 
    class default
    end select

  end subroutine test_list_free

end module fortuno_basetypes
```

When running
```
❯ lfortran --tree -v -c basetypes.f90
```
it triggers 
```
zsh: segmentation fault (core dumped)  lfortran -v -c basetypes.f90
```
I am using the LFortran from conda-forge:
```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
Please note, that the code snippet above is the minimal working example I could come up with and is not supposed to deliver something useable (but is standard conforming IMO). See the [complete source file](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno/blob/672a3981f6b0200d03ccce287c7722c5172e9636/src/fortuno/basetypes.f90) for further details.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2513841831","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515#issuecomment-2513841831","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515","id":2513841831,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6V1i6n","user":{"login":"aradi","id":2452321,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0NTIzMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2452321?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi","html_url":"https://github.com/aradi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-03T08:23:20Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T08:23:20Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@kmr-srbh Perfect, thanks a lot for digging deeper. Is there anything I can do more for promoting the bug fix process? I'd find it really cool, if we could compile Fortuno with LFortran as well, and I guess, it would be also a kind of milestone for LFortran itself :wink:  (But I can understand, if that is not your highest priority at the moment...)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2513841831/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

If I do it, the compiler builds, but when I try to compile a source file with it, I get an error message about missing `clang` compiler:
```
sh: clang: command not found                                                     
The command 'clang -o hello.out hello.tmp.o  -L"/mnt/scratch/aradi/BUILD/lfortran
/src/bin/../runtime" -Wl,-rpath,"/mnt/scratch/aradi/BUILD/lfortran/src/bin/../run
time" -llfortran_runtime -lm' failed.
```
I think, one should either declare in the documentation, that a `clang` compiler is needed (not just any working C++ compiler), or, even better, add clang as an additional to be installed dependency when creating the conda environment.

Note: This is the same issue as reported for the conda-forge recipe at conda-forge/lfortran-feedstock#110

+{% set name = "fortuno" %}
+{% set version = "0.1.0" %}
+{% set build = 0 %}
+{% set mpi = mpi or "nompi" %}
+
+package:
+  name: {{ name|lower }}
+  version: {{ version }}
+
+source:
+  url: https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno/archive/v{{ version }}.tar.gz
+  sha256: 9639be1159e03d5e31032af6aa9d4f8483d1f3df0b84a3b43a564273a68ff5e1
+
+build:
+  number: {{ build }}
+  skip: True  # [win]
+
+  {% if mpi != "nompi" %}
+  {% set mpi_prefix = "mpi_" + mpi %}
+  {% else %}
+  {% set mpi_prefix = "nompi" %}
+  {% endif %}
+  string: "{{ mpi_prefix }}_h{{ PKG_HASH }}_{{ build }}"
+
+  {% if mpi != "nompi" %}
+  run_exports:
+    - {{ name }} * {{ mpi_prefix }}_*
+  {% endif %}
+
+requirements:
+  build:
+    - {{ compiler('fortran') Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| Go              | `@conda-forge/help-go`        |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

Checklist
- [X] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [X] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [X] Source is from official source.
- [X] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [ ] ~If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.~
- [X] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [X] Build number is 0.
- [X] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [X] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [X] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| Go              | `@conda-forge/help-go`        |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

Checklist
- [X] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [X] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [X] Source is from official source.
- [X] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [ ] ~If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.~
- [X] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [X] Build number is 0.
- [X] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [X] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [X] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

Description

Build of any arbitrary project with MPI-dependency fails when using oneAPI 2025.0.

Executing
```
fpm new test
cd test
echo -e "[dependencies]\nmpi =\"*\"" >> fpm.toml
FPM_FC=ifx fpm build
```
results in
```
<ERROR> *cmd_build* Model error: local INTEL MPI library does not support -v
STOP 1
```

Note: oneAPI 2024.2 seems to work.

### Expected Behaviour

Building the project as expected without error message.

### Version of fpm

0.10.1

### Platform and Architecture

Linux/x86_64

### Additional Information

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| Go              | `@conda-forge/help-go`        |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

- [X] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".

- [ ] Source is from official source.
- [X] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).

- [X] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [X] Build number is 0.

- [X] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [X] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| Rust            | `@conda-forge/help-rust`      |
| Go              | `@conda-forge/help-go`        |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io

All apologies in advance if your recipe PR does not receive prompt attention.
This is a high volume repository and the reviewers are volunteers. Review times
vary depending on the number of reviewers on a given language team and may be days
or weeks. We are always looking for more staged-recipe reviewers. If you are
interested in volunteering, please contact a member of @conda-forge/core.
We'd love to have the help!
-->

Checklist
- [X] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [X] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [X] Source is from official source.
- [X] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [ ] ~If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.~
- [X] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [X] Build number is 0.
- [X] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [X] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [X] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

I thought, I'll test, how far I get with it to build the [Fortuno unit testing](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno) framework, to give further impulses for the development. :smile: 

I encountered a segfault when compiling the a given source file. The kind of minimal reproducer seems to be:
```fortran
module fortuno_basetypes
  implicit none

  type, abstract :: test_base
    character(:), allocatable :: name
  end type test_base


  type :: test_ptr_item
    class(test_base), pointer :: item => null()                                 
  end type test_ptr_item


  type :: test_list
    private
    type(test_ptr_item), pointer :: storage_(:) => null()                       
    integer :: nitems = 0
  contains
    procedure :: free => test_list_free
  end type


contains

  subroutine test_list_free(this)
    class(test_list), intent(inout) :: this                                     

    select type (item => this%storage_(1)%item)                                 
    class default
    end select

  end subroutine test_list_free

end module fortuno_basetypes
```

When running
```
❯ lfortran --tree -v -c basetypes.f90
```
it triggers 
```
zsh: segmentation fault (core dumped)  lfortran -v -c basetypes.f90
```
I am using the LFortran from conda-forge:
```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
Please note, that the code snippet above is the minimal working example I could come up with and is not supposed to deliver something useable (but is standard conforming IMO). See the [complete source file](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno/blob/672a3981f6b0200d03ccce287c7722c5172e9636/src/fortuno/basetypes.f90) for further details.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [x] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

When I install lfortran via conda on x86_64/Linux, it can not link programs due to missing clang.

Reproducer:
```
conda create -y -n lfortran.test
conda activate lfortran.test
conda install -y lfortran
echo "print *, 'Hello'; end" > hello.f90
lfortran hello.f90
```
delivers
```
sh: line 1: clang: command not found
The command 'clang -o hello.out hello.tmp.o  -L".../miniforge3/envs/lfortran.test/bin/../share/lfortran/lib" -Wl,-rpath,".../miniforge3/envs/lfortran.test/bin/../share/lfortran/lib" -llfortran_runtime -lm' failed.
```
Note: If I install clang alongside with the lfortran package, things work as supposed.

The compiler flags used by fpm when building with the Intel oneAPI compiler prevent the successful build of MPI-parallelized code using IntelMPI.

To reproduce:
Create a standard fpm project with
```
fpm new test
```
modify fpm.toml to contain
```
[fortran]
implicit-typing = true
implicit-external = true
source-form = "free"

[dependencies]
mpi = "*"
```
and modify the library source file as follows
 ```
module test
  use mpi_f08, only : MPI_COMM_WORLD, mpi_barrier, mpi_init, mpi_finalize
  implicit none
  private

  public :: say_hello
contains
  subroutine say_hello
    call mpi_init()
    call MPI_Barrier(MPI_COMM_WORLD)
    print *, "Hello, test!"
    call mpi_finalize()
  end subroutine say_hello
end module test
```
and build it with
```
FPM_FC=ifx fpm build
```
Using `ifx (IFX) 2024.1.0 20240308` I obtain the error message:
```
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o): in function `test_MP_say_hello_':
/media/aradi/ramdisk/test/././src/test.f90:10:(.text+0x2c): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o):(.debug_info+0x3e): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
```

### Expected Behaviour

It would be desirable, especially for less experienced programmers, that MPI-parallel code compiles with the oneAPI compiler automatically,

### Version of fpm

0.10.1, alpha

### Platform and Architecture

Linux

### Additional Information

The culprit is the `-standard-semantics` option. Using this option changes the name mangling, and the IntelMPI fortran modules apparently use the "original" name mangling. Issuing
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-standard-semantics" fpm build
```
generates the same error. Using
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-stand f18" fpm build
```

The compiler flags used by fpm when building with the Intel oneAPI compiler prevent the successful build of MPI-parallelized code using IntelMPI.

To reproduce:
Create a standard fpm project with
```
fpm new test
```
modify fpm.toml to contain
```
[fortran]
implicit-typing = true
implicit-external = true
source-form = "free"

[dependencies]
mpi = "*"
```
and modify the library source file as follows
 ```
module test
  use mpi_f08, only : MPI_COMM_WORLD, mpi_barrier, mpi_init, mpi_finalize
  implicit none
  private

  public :: say_hello
contains
  subroutine say_hello
    call mpi_init()
    call MPI_Barrier(MPI_COMM_WORLD)
    print *, "Hello, test!"
    call mpi_finalize()
  end subroutine say_hello
end module test
```
and build it with
```
FPM_FC=ifx fpm build
```
Using `ifx (IFX) 2024.1.0 20240308` I obtain the error message:
```
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o): in function `test_MP_say_hello_':
/media/aradi/ramdisk/test/././src/test.f90:10:(.text+0x2c): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o):(.debug_info+0x3e): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
```

### Expected Behaviour

It would be desirable, especially for less experienced programmers, that MPI-parallel code compiles with the oneAPI compiler automatically,

### Version of fpm

0.10.1, alpha

### Platform and Architecture

Linux

### Additional Information

The culprit is the `-standard-semantics` option. Using this option changes the name mangling, and the IntelMPI fortran modules apparently use the "original" name mangling. Issuing
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-standard-semantics" fpm build
```
generates the same error. Using
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-stand f18" fpm build
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/1033","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/1033/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/1033/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/1033/events","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/fpm/issues/1033","id":2304702580,"node_id":"I_kwDODe7zws6JXvh0","number":1033,"title":"App is not installed if source is not main.f90","user":{"login":"aradi","id":2452321,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0NTIzMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2452321?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi","html_url":"https://github.com/aradi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1786237147,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzg2MjM3MTQ3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/fpm/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"d73a4a","default":true,"description":"Something isn't working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-05-19T17:14:44Z","updated_at":"2024-05-19T17:14:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description

I have created a [generic cookiecutter template for Fortran projects](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/cookiecutter-fortran-project) offering various modern build systems as option, among others also fpm. When trying to get the same repository layout for all build systems, which differs slightly from fpm's default layout, I stumbled about a strange behavior upon app installation:

* If the app source is `main.f90`, everything works as expected, app installed.
* If the app source is something else (e.g. `myapp.f90`), an error is raised upon installation (`<ERROR> Project does not contain any installable targets`)
* Strange enough, if there are two programs in the app folder and one of them is in `main.f90`, then both apps are installed.

```
fpm new test
cd test
mv app/main.f90 app/myapp.f90
fpm build
fpm install --prefix $PWD/_install
```

### Expected Behaviour

The code above should install the program `myapp` into `_install/bin`

### Version of fpm

0.10.1

### Platform and Architecture

Linux

The compiler flags used by fpm when building with the Intel oneAPI compiler prevent the successful build of MPI-parallelized code using IntelMPI.

To reproduce:
Create a standard fpm project with
```
fpm new test
```
modify fpm.toml to contain
```
[fortran]
implicit-typing = true
implicit-external = true
source-form = "free"

[dependencies]
mpi = "*"
```
and modify the library source file as follows
 ```
module test
  use mpi_f08, only : MPI_COMM_WORLD, mpi_barrier, mpi_init, mpi_finalize
  implicit none
  private

  public :: say_hello
contains
  subroutine say_hello
    call mpi_init()
    call MPI_Barrier(MPI_COMM_WORLD)
    print *, "Hello, test!"
    call mpi_finalize()
  end subroutine say_hello
end module test
```
and build it with
```
FPM_FC=ifx fpm build
```
Using `ifx (IFX) 2024.1.0 20240308` I obtain the error message:
```
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o): in function `test_MP_say_hello_':
/media/aradi/ramdisk/test/././src/test.f90:10:(.text+0x2c): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o):(.debug_info+0x3e): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
```

### Expected Behaviour

It would be desirable, especially for less experienced programmers, that MPI-parallel code compiles with the oneAPI compiler automatically,

### Version of fpm

0.10.1, alpha

### Platform and Architecture

Linux

### Additional Information

The culprit is the `-standard-semantics` option. Using this option changes the name mangling, and the IntelMPI fortran modules apparently use the "original" name mangling. Issuing
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-standard-semantics" fpm build
```
generates the same error. Using
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-stand f18" fpm build
```

```
The problem is not the linking of the MPI libraries or any MPI related flag. The problem is the `-standard-semantics` flag, which I guess comes from fpm, maybe from the `get_release_compile_flags()` routine in `fpm_compiler.f90`.

As far as I understand, using `-standard-semantics` when compiling a source, changes how the module names, module procedure names and module variable names are mangled into linker symbols (`*_mod_*` vs. `*_MOD_*`). Consequently, you can only link together module based libraries, where either all components were compiled with `-standard-semantics` or all without. Apparently, Intel decided to compile its mpi_f08 interface without that flag, so it can be only linked to Fortran sources, which do not use this flag either. (We had a lot of troubles with that flag earlier exactly for this reason in DFTB+, and dropped at some point).

In earlier times, it was needed because otherwise automatic left-hand-side allocation did not work. But one could work around it by using `-assume lhs_realloc` instead. Latter does not change the name-mangling, and still ensures the correct automatic allocation. Actually, in recent ifx/ifort compilers `-assume lhs_realloc` is the default already, so no explicit arguments are needed for automatic LHS reallocation any more.

So, unless it is some other weird feature covered by `-standard-semantics` which is needed to build fpm, I'd rather suggest to use `-assume lhs_realloc` instead. The resulting object files could be then linked against IntelMPI, and we won't need any additional compiler-specific entry in `fpm.toml`.

    call mpi_finalize()
  end subroutine say_hello
end module test
```
and build it with
```
FPM_FC=ifx fpm build
```
Using `ifx (IFX) 2024.1.0 20240308` I obtain the error message:
```
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o): in function `test_MP_say_hello_':
/media/aradi/ramdisk/test/././src/test.f90:10:(.text+0x2c): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
ld: build/ifx_434F714D4D7C6A1A/test/libtest.a(src_test.f90.o):(.debug_info+0x3e): undefined reference to `mpi_f08_compile_constants_MP_mpi_comm_world_'
```

### Expected Behaviour

It would be desirable, especially for less experienced programmers, that MPI-parallel code compiles with the oneAPI compiler automatically,

### Version of fpm

0.10.1, alpha

### Platform and Architecture

Linux

### Additional Information

The culprit is the `-standard-semantics` option. Using this option changes the name mangling, and the IntelMPI fortran modules apparently use the "original" name mangling. Issuing
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-standard-semantics" fpm build
```
generates the same error. Using
```
FPM_FC=ifx FPM_FFLAGS="-stand f18" fpm build
```

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

The `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` include file of the Fortran compiler is missing in the package.

We had a similar issue for the compilers on Linux, which had been fixed (https://github.com/conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock/pull/123). We would probably need a similar fix for the osx platforms to be able to proceed to build MPICH with Fortran 2008 MPI-interface (https://github.com/conda-forge/mpich-feedstock/pull/89).

### Installed packages

```shell
-- Unfortunately, I can not install conda on osx due to lack of appropriate hardware --
```

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-06T16:10:11Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T16:10:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

Duplicate of [conda-forge/gfortran_osx64-feedstock#55](https://github.com/conda-forge/gfortran_osx-64-feedstock/issues/55), but I don't know, which feedstock is the right one to report it.

The `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` seems to be missing in the compiler package. This prevents the compilation of moder Fortran/C interface implementations, among others, the build of the MPI-F08 interface in the MPICH-package (https://github.com/conda-forge/mpich-feedstock/pull/89).

My knowledge is too limited to proceed. I've added a test in #79 for the missing file, which brings some of the builds to fail, but I don't know, how to fix the problem. Any help would be appreciated, as we can not build an MPICH with MPI-F08 on OSX, and consequently, modern MPI-parallalized Fortran packages won't be able to use the MPICH framework on Conda.

Any help would be appreciated

### Installed packages

```shell
-- Unfortunately, I can not install conda on osx due to lack of appropriate hardware --
```

```

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

Duplicate of [conda-forge/gfortran_osx64-feedstock#55](https://github.com/conda-forge/gfortran_osx-64-feedstock/issues/55), but I don't know, which feedstock is the right one to report it.

The `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` seems to be missing in the compiler package. This prevents the compilation of moder Fortran/C interface implementations, among others, the build of the MPI-F08 interface in the MPICH-package (https://github.com/conda-forge/mpich-feedstock/pull/89).

My knowledge is too limited to proceed. I've added a test in #79 for the missing file, which brings some of the builds to fail, but I don't know, how to fix the problem. Any help would be appreciated, as we can not build an MPICH with MPI-F08 on OSX, and consequently, modern MPI-parallalized Fortran packages won't be able to use the MPICH framework on Conda.

Any help would be appreciated

### Installed packages

-- Unfortunately, I can not install conda on osx due to lack of appropriate hardware --
```

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

The `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` include file of the Fortran compiler is missing in the package.

We had a similar issue for the compilers on Linux, which had been fixed (https://github.com/conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock/pull/123). We would probably need a similar fix for the osx platforms to be able to proceed to build MPICH with Fortran 2008 MPI-interface (https://github.com/conda-forge/mpich-feedstock/pull/89).

Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [X] ~Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed)~ * [X] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [X] Ensured the license file is being packaged. After a longer odyssey, this PR is basically rebuilding MPICH using the latest (fixed versions) of the gfortran compiler. MPICH should now detect "F2008 support" (which is actually F2018 support) on all platforms, and consequently provide the `mpi_f08` interface everywhere. The build script uses now `cp` instead of `ln` to put the `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` into the right search folder on OSX, as `cp` (in contrast to `ln`) fails reliably, if the file is not latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [X] Ensured the license file is being packaged. mpifx was fixed to minor version number when used as dependency. This lead to problems for the DFTB+ package, where the direct mpifx dependency clashed with the indirect one via libnegf as they required different minor version numbers. Since MpiFx is semantically versioned, pinning major version number should be enough. Checklist * [X] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [X] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [X] ~Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed)~ * [X] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the version number when used as dependency. This lead to problems for the DFTB+ package, where the direct mpifx dependency clashed with the indirect one via libnegf as they required different minor version numbers. Since MpiFx is semantically versioned, pinning major version number should be enough. working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-12-01T10:13:01Z","updated_at":"2024-01-26T08:22:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

When installing a Fortran compiler (either via the `fortran-compiler` package as user or via `{{ compiler('fortran') }}` in a recipe), the C-include file `include/ISO_Fortran_binding.h` is not installed. Missing this file prevents the compilation of modern Fortran-C interfaces. It also prevents autotools from recognizing the Fortran compiler (gfortran) being F2008-compliant. For example, this seems to be the reason, why MPICH does not build the `mpi_f08` module (conda-forge/mpich-feedstock#84) preventing the usage of the modern MPI-interface in Fortran programs.

If I build gcc myself, the include file gets installed. (I use the `--enable-languages=c,c++,fortran` option).

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

Fortran 2008 interface (`mpi_f08`) not available in the Conda packaged version of MPICH.

Trying to compile the following code:
```fortran
program test
  use mpi_f08
end program test
```
with
```
mpifort test.f90
```
results in
```
test.f90:2:7:

    2 |   use mpi_f08
      |       1
Fatal Error: Cannot open module file 'mpi_f08.mod' for reading at (1): No such file or directory
compilation terminated.
```
The root cause could be eventually conda-forge/compilers-feedstock#59, but not sure, how to change the situation. (Due to this issue, I unfortunately will have to drop the MPICH-support of my software package on Conda and offer only OpenMPI, which supports the F08-interface on Conda.)

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

When installing a Fortran compiler (either via the `fortran-compiler` package as user or via `{{ compiler('fortran') }}` in a recipe), the C-include file `include/ISO_Fortran_binding.h` is not installed. Missing this file prevents the compilation of modern Fortran-C interfaces. It also prevents autotools from recognizing the Fortran compiler (gfortran) being F2008-compliant. For example, this seems to be the reason, why MPICH does not build the `mpi_f08` module (conda-forge/mpich-feedstock#84) preventing the usage of the modern MPI-interface in Fortran programs.

If I build gcc myself, the include file gets installed. (I use the `--enable-languages=c,c++,fortran` option).

Adds support for the Fortran 2008 interface for MPICH in Conda. Now, after the appropriate fix in the GNU-compiler (conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123), this finally seems to be possible. This PR explicitly enables the F2008 interface build and tests the F2008 interface. Adds support for the Fortran 2008 interface for MPICH in Conda. Now, after the appropriate fix in the GNU-compiler (conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123), this finally seems to be possible. This PR explicitly enables the F2008 interface build and tests the F2008 interface. Adds support for the Fortran 2008 interface for MPICH in Conda. Now, after the appropriate fix in the GNU-compiler (conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123), this finally seems to be possible. This PR explicitly enables the F2008 interface build and tests the F2008 interface. requested"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-01-28T09:36:22Z","updated_at":"2024-01-28T17:58:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Comment:

As I have no access to Mac hardware, I can not check myself. But it seems to me, that the missing `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` issue for the gfortran compilers, which was fixed in conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123 for Linux, is still available on osx_64. At least, the building of MPICH with modern Fortran interfaces, which requires this include file, seems to be successful now on all platforms, except osx_64, where the compiler capability detection fails due to the missing include file (conda-forge/mpich-feedstock#84).

Could you please, check, whether the osx_64 Conda package contains that include file, and make a similar fix to conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123, if not? (@isuruf as you fixed the other package, could you give this a look?)

Apparently, the MPICH configure script can not detect the F2008 support of the compiler (`2024-01-28T16:03:42.0027680Z checking for Fortran 2008 support... no`). I had the same problem with the Linux compilers before, which I could then track down to the missing `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` in the compiler packages. Once this had been fixed in conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123, the MPICH configure script worked correctly with those compilers.

So, that means, that the file is there, it is just not found by the C-compiler (I've completely missed the fact, that osx uses clang instead of gcc). Two comments from my side: * What about just passing the correct `-I` flag with the `include/` directory of the Fortran compiler to the C-compiler (in `CFLAGS` or so) instead of symlinking the .h file? * Yes, I definitely care about the F2008 interface in both MPI packages in Conda. :smile: However, I am not sure, whether the problem we had must be fixed in the compiler packages. IMO a C compiler should not necessarily have to see the include directory of an other (Fortran) compiler by default. Instead is OK to expect the user to pass the appropriate `-I` option, if an include directory outside of the ecosystem of the compiler should be searched for `.h` files. In our case, that would mean, that we have to pass the appropriate `-I` flag to the configure script in some form. (Actually, it would be even OK, if that would be needed for the gfortran/gcc combination, but there, since it is the same compiler, it is not needed.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/mpich-feedstock/issues/comments/1915329093/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Comment:

As I have no access to Mac hardware, I can not check myself. But it seems to me, that the missing `ISO_Fortran_binding.h` issue for the gfortran compilers, which was fixed in conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123 for Linux, is still available on osx_64. At least, the building of MPICH with modern Fortran interfaces, which requires this include file, seems to be successful now on all platforms, except osx_64, where the compiler capability detection fails due to the missing include file (conda-forge/mpich-feedstock#84).

Could you please, check, whether the osx_64 Conda package contains that include file, and make a similar fix to conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#123, if not? (@isuruf as you fixed the other package, could you give this a look?)

(Also, this include file can be Fortran compiler and even Fortran compiler version dependent, so it should really stay with the Fortran compiler.) Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

Fortran 2008 interface (`mpi_f08`) not available in the Conda packaged version of MPICH.

Trying to compile the following code:
```fortran
program test
  use mpi_f08
end program test
```
with
```
mpifort test.f90
```
results in
```
test.f90:2:7:

    2 |   use mpi_f08
      |       1
Fatal Error: Cannot open module file 'mpi_f08.mod' for reading at (1): No such file or directory
compilation terminated.
```
The root cause could be eventually conda-forge/ctng-compilers-feedstock#122, but not sure, how to change the situation. (Due to this issue, I unfortunately will have to drop the MPICH-support of my software package on Conda and offer only OpenMPI, which supports the F08-interface on Conda.)

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-12-01T10:13:01Z","updated_at":"2023-12-01T10:13:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

When installing a Fortran compiler (either via the `fortran-compiler` package as user or via `{{ compiler('fortran') }}` in a recipe), the C-include file `include/ISO_Fortran_binding.h` is not installed. Missing this file prevents the compilation of modern Fortran-C interfaces. It also prevents autotools from recognizing the Fortran compiler (gfortran) being F2008-compliant. For example, this seems to be the reason, why MPICH does not build the `mpi_f08` module (conda-forge/mpich-feedstock#84) preventing the usage of the modern MPI-interface in Fortran programs.

If I build gcc myself, the include file gets installed. (I use the `--enable-languages=c,c++,fortran` option).

| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the

A quick question, as I am not 100% familiar with dependency pinning and the `pin_subpackage()` directive (I find the corresponding Conda docs rather confusing....). Does
```
build:
  run_exports:
    - {{ pin_subpackage(name, max_pin='x.x') }} {{ mpi_prefix }}_*
```
ensure, that all dependencies (especially libomm, matrixswitch, elpa and ntpoly) are pinned in their major and minor versions? Elsi 2.8.* is broken on Conda (it did not have this `pin_subpackage` option),  because it pulls now NTPoly 3.0 (instead of 2.9), which has incompatible changes. I just wish to get confirmed, that using the option above this can not happen again. (Is this equivalent to adding individual `pin_compatible()` settings in the `run` section for each of those components?)

Comment:

A quick question, as I am not 100% familiar with dependency pinning and the `pin_subpackage()` directive (I find the corresponding Conda docs rather confusing....). Does
```
build:
  run_exports:
    - {{ pin_subpackage(name, max_pin='x.x') }} {{ mpi_prefix }}_*
```
ensure, that all dependencies (especially libomm, matrixswitch, elpa and ntpoly) are pinned in their major and minor versions? Elsi 2.8.* is broken on Conda (it did not have this `pin_subpackage` option),  because it pulls now NTPoly 3.0 (instead of 2.9), which has incompatible changes. I just wish to get confirmed, that using the option above this can not happen again. (Is this equivalent to adding individual `pin_compatible()` settings in the `run` section for each of those components?)

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/363","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/363/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/363/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/363/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/363","id":1338730585,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc5Py2hZ","number":363,"title":"Create pedantic/strict mode","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2022-08-15T09:07:32Z","updated_at":"2023-02-14T19:24:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"*Original issue*: https://gitlab.com/lfortran/lfortran/-/issues/450

In this mode, LFortran would provide warnings (or even errors) for code that is valid, but we determined as a community that we would not like using it.

Examples:

* more restrictive intents: it can force `intent(inout)` to `intent(in)` or `intent(out)` where appropriate; and require `intent` to be present for all arguments
* apply `pure` and `simple` where appropriate
* implicit typing, implied save and other such features would not be allowed
* "1.3" and "1.3d0" would not be allowed, you have to specify the kind such as "1.3_dp" or "1.3_sp"
* `endif`, `enddo` etc. would not be allowed, you have to use `end if`, `end do`, ...
* `real(8)` and `real*8` would not be allowed, you have to use `real(dp)` and such
* standardize how to define return type of a function (see [here](https://fortran-lang.discourse.group/t/closing-if-loops-by-endif-vs-end-if/1651/7))
* not allow routines without explicit interfaces (i.e., all routines must be in modules or have an interface specified).
* When a variable is used that is not initialized, it will give a compiler error (obviously we have to ensure no false positives)
* Enforce `target` for arguments if the parameter has a `target` attribute (https://fortran-lang.discourse.group/t/is-pointing-to-a-target-dummy-argument-safe-after-return/5199/8)
* ...

We can add any number of rules. The idea is that we determine as a community what kind of modern Fortran we would like to be writing and then use the compiler to enforce the rules. It can also be configurable.

This mode will be possible to enable / disable with a compiler option. First this mode would be off by default, but as people use it and if they like it, we can discuss if it should be on by default (you'll always be able to turn it off with an option).

See the original GitLab issue for more ideas. support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

port/issues/591","number":591,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Adds support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

port/issues/591","number":591,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Adds support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

support","user":{"login":"gnikit","id":16143716,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTQzNzE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16143716?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit","html_url":"https://github.com/gnikit","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gnikit/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"feat: add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---
