add basic support for `fypp`

This is the start of incorporating fypp into VS Code

Hi @aradi I have gone ahead and added the simplest solution I could think off for `fypp` support to get the ball rolling and then,
I thought it would be a good idea to discuss some of the functionality we would want to include and problems I/we will have to address. I will start listing listing some of the things I have already thought off:

## TODO

- [x] Allow for arguments to be passed to `fypp`
- [x] Incorporate the existing `IncludeDirs` passed to linters as `--include=${}` in fypp
- [x] Implement an `fypp` interface to VS Code settings. For the preprocessor defines it makes sense we share them with `fortls` too. Have 2 settings and merge them in the backend for `fortls` or have a single option for both? I think the first one is better.
- [x] Add `fypp` auto-download with pip
- [x] Improve the compiler flags passed into the linter when `fypp` is enabled
- [x] Add better safety checks for other compilers or add the appropriate compiler flags for `fypp` to work
- [x] The linter serves diagnostics on the "expanded" source file we need to find a way to map back to the "compressed" file, i.e. pre-fypp to serve the diagnostics there. I don't know what resources does `fypp` provide to make that possible.
     >I think the solution to this is use `--line-numbering`, at least for me that works.
- [x] determine if Fixed or Free form
- [ ] Add unittests
- [ ] Add integration test for fypp
- [ ] Should the preprocessor definitions for `fypp` and `fortls` be merged? `fortls` understands normal Fortran preprocessor only. (I would like to discuss swapping to `fypp` if it can also handle normal preprocessor directives).
- [ ] Add support for `ifort` and `nagfor` if possible.

---

Actually, fypp does not have a proper lexer/tokenizer, it only looks for simple patterns via regular expressions. Most of the content is then passed to Python, which will parse then the expressions using its own parser. If you want to implement cpp-grammar, things get more complicated, as you would have to write a proper lexer and expression parser, so I think, it won't really profit from anything within fypp. I know that [PreForM](https://github.com/szaghi/PreForM) can deal with a bunch of the cpp-constructs, but not sure, whether that is of any help for you.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support/issues/comments/1205485895/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

A few notes:
- I am definitely in favor of avoiding intermediate files, whenever possible.
- GFortran seems to be able to process source on stdin:
  ```
  echo -e "print *, 'hello'\nend\n" | gfortran -x f95 -
  ```
- Independent of the source handling, one should probably anyway consider to set the `-J` option for gfortran, in order to collect all modfiles at one place in a clever way. I am not sure, what the right strategy is, I just find somewhat disturbing, when `.mod` and `.smod` files pop up in the source directory. (But this is clearly not an fypp related issue)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/vscode-fortran-support/issues/comments/1193919902/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Now that hash functions have been added to the standard library, it is time to add hash maps. Currently this PR only includes the documentation, `doc/specs/stdlib_hash_maps.md`, giving the proposed API. The proposed API has three module files: `stdlib_key_data_wrapper.f90`, which provides wrappers for the key and data components of the entries and some of the hash function; `stdlib_chaining_hash_map.f90`, which implements a chaining hash map with linked lists; and `stdlib_open_hash_maps.f90`, which implements a linear addressing open hashing map. After the (modified) API  has been approved by two people I will add the required module files.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/611/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/611/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/comments/1006062350","html_url":"https://github.com/fortran-lang/stdlib/pull/611#issuecomment-1006062350","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/fortran-lang/stdlib/issues/611","id":1006062350,"node_id":"IC_kwDODZiDws4790sO","user":{"login":"aradi","id":2452321,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0NTIzMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2452321?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi","html_url":"https://github.com/aradi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/aradi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-05T20:42:14Z","updated_at":"2022-01-05T20:42:14Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I am lacking the time to make a proper review, but I could not resist to make few general comments about containers, just in case you find it useful:

* One should not rely on array of integers (or characters) as data type, especially not for the value. Transfering anything else than intrinsic types via `transfer()` to integers and back can lead to unpredictable behaviour IMO. Think about a derived type with allocated pointer components. You transfer it to integer representation and put in in the hash-map. What does the map contain? The full data of the type instance? Or only the pointer descriptors? And what happens if the original type instance is finalized, and then you read out the integer representation from the hash-map and transfer it back? Would the pointers within the retrieved instance point to something meaningful? (likely not). Using `class(*)` instead is more robust, although with run-time penalties and the annoying `select type` everywhere. Users would have to write wrappers for all their custom types they would like to put into the container. `c_loc()` is basically like transfer, in the sense, that you do not get compile-time (and possible not even immediate run-time errors), when converting back the retrieved item to the wrong data type.

* I think, making internal details of the implementation of the map (like an integer index) of the public API would constrain future changes of the map-implementation. Also, how long is the integer index valid? Would it change, in case the hash table must grow? I think, returning a pointer to the data (as proposed above) is somewhat more robust, but it should be still well documented, whether there are any map operations, which make the pointer (returned earlier) invalid.

* Unfortunately, whatever you do, you will not be able to fix the biggest problem of Fortran: the lack of templates/generic programming. If you wanted robust, efficient and easy to use containers with **compile-time** errors for many easy mistakes (like putting in an integer and reading it out as a real), you would need container templates (macros), which the user can instantiate with the actual type. There are already examples using cpp (see https://github.com/SCM-NV/ftl), and one can do it even much nicer with fypp :wink: (see https://github.com/dftbplus/dftbplus/blob/master/src/dftbp/include/allocatablelist.fypp for a simple list template and https://github.com/dftbplus/dftbplus/blob/master/src/dftbp/dftb/repulsive/repulsivelist.F90 for an instantiation with a complex derived type). But, unfortunately, that would require the consumers of stdlib to use fypp, which is against the design principle of stdlib. :disappointed:
